claims.claim_text: The CKAD exam explicitly covers implementing probes and health checks under Application Observability and Maintenance.
claims.rationale: This is an official curriculum/exam-scope claim and the Linux Foundation CKAD page names probes and health checks as a domain competency.

claims.claim_text: Liveness failures restart containers, while readiness failures leave containers running but stop Service traffic to them.
claims.span_hint: section: The Mental Model: Three Questions, Three Actions
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes v1.35 probe docs directly define the action taken for liveness and readiness failures.

claims.claim_text: Repeated liveness failures can produce restart-loop behavior that surfaces as CrashLoopBackOff.
claims.span_hint: section: The Mental Model: Three Questions, Three Actions
claims.rationale: The probe docs establish that liveness triggers restarts, and the Pod lifecycle docs define CrashLoopBackOff as repeated failed restarts.

claims.claim_text: Startup probes disable liveness and readiness checks until startup succeeds, and they are the intended way to protect slow-starting containers instead of using very large liveness delays.
claims.span_hint: section: Startup Probe: Protect Slow Boot Without Weakening Liveness Forever
claims.rationale: The v1.35 docs explicitly describe startupProbe gating and recommend it instead of long liveness startup intervals.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes 1.35 supports four probe mechanisms: HTTP GET, TCP socket, exec, and gRPC.
claims.span_hint: section: Probe Mechanisms: How Kubernetes Checks Health
claims.rationale: The versioned Kubernetes 1.35 probes page enumerates these four mechanisms.

claims.claim_text: HTTP probes treat status codes 200 through 399 as success, so a redirecting endpoint can appear healthy.
claims.rationale: The official probe docs define HTTP success as status >=200 and <400 and document redirect handling.

claims.claim_text: A TCP probe only verifies that the port is open; it does not validate higher-level application protocol behavior.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes probe docs define TCP success as opening the port, which is weaker than an application-level check.

claims.claim_text: Exec probes run a command inside the container, succeed only on exit status 0, and add process-creation overhead on each run.
claims.rationale: The official docs describe exec success semantics and explicitly warn about per-execution process overhead.

claims.claim_text: gRPC probes require the gRPC Health Checking Protocol and replace the older exec-probe workaround that bundled a separate probing binary.
claims.rationale: The concept docs define the protocol requirement, and the Kubernetes blog explains the prior grpc-health-probe executable workaround and the native replacement.

claims.claim_text: Probe defaults and constraints include initialDelaySeconds=0, periodSeconds=10, timeoutSeconds=1, failureThreshold=3, and successThreshold must remain 1 for liveness and startup probes.
claims.span_hint: section: Timing Parameters: Fast Recovery Without False Positives
claims.rationale: These are exact documented Kubernetes probe defaults and field constraints.

claims.claim_text: The module's startup-budget rule of thumb uses initialDelaySeconds + failureThreshold * periodSeconds, and Kubernetes recommends a startupProbe when normal startup exceeds that budget.
claims.span_hint: section: Timing Parameters: Fast Recovery Without False Positives
claims.rationale: The v1.35 docs use this exact formula in their startupProbe guidance.

claims.claim_text: A Pod can be in Running phase but not yet Ready, and Pods that are not Ready are removed from Service endpoints/load-balancing pools.
claims.span_hint: section: Debugging Readiness and Service Endpoints
claims.rationale: The Pod conditions docs explicitly distinguish Running from Ready and state that unready Pods are removed from Service endpoints.

claims.claim_text: Service backends are selected by Pod labels, so missing endpoints can be caused by selector/label mismatch as well as readiness failure.
claims.span_hint: section: Debugging Readiness and Service Endpoints
claims.rationale: The labels and selectors docs state that a Service targets Pods through its label selector.

claims.claim_text: Probe fields on a directly managed Pod are generally not mutable in place, so changing probe configuration usually requires recreating or replacing the Pod.
claims.span_hint: section: Worked Example: Fix a Restart Loop Caused by Liveness
claims.rationale: The Pods docs list the small set of mutable Pod fields, and probe configuration is not among them.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes v1.35: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the canonical v1.35 reference for probe semantics, mechanisms, defaults, and failure behavior.

further_reading.title: Configure Liveness, Readiness and Startup Probes
further_reading.why_relevant: This task page provides runnable examples and concrete event output for probe troubleshooting.

further_reading.title: Pod Conditions
further_reading.why_relevant: It clarifies the difference between Running and Ready and explains why readiness affects Service endpoint membership.

further_reading.title: Certified Kubernetes Application Developer (CKAD)
further_reading.why_relevant: It shows the official CKAD v1.35 domain coverage, including probes and health checks.

notes: Source map: S001=v1.35 probes concept doc; S002=Pods concept doc; S003=Pod Conditions; S004=Kubernetes blog on native gRPC probes; S005=Labels and Selectors; S006=Pod Lifecycle; S007=Linux Foundation CKAD page. Excluded analogies, quiz prompts, hands-on exercise steps, and code blocks; only hard factual prose and table-default claims were seeded.
